criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the theoretical framework of operator-based interpolants and multipurpose drifts.
The agent has read Definition 2.1 to understand the operator-based interpolant I(α,β) = αx₀ + βx₁ as a generalization of stochastic interpolants.
The agent has read Lemma 2.3 to understand that the drifts η₀ and η₁ are jointly optimized via separate quadratic regression objectives.
The plan includes implementing the operator-based interpolant I(α,β) = αx₀ + βx₁ as defined in Equation 1.
The plan includes implementing the dual drift network predicting both η₀ and η₁ simultaneously with a shared or dual-headed neural network.
The plan includes implementing the training loss as the sum of two MSE objectives: one for predicting x₀ from I(α,β) and one for predicting x₁ from I(α,β).
The code for the operator-based interpolant I(α,β) = αx₀ + βx₁ has been implemented, supporting general matrix-valued α and β operators.
The code for the multipurpose drifts η₀(α,β,x) and η₁(α,β,x) as conditional expectations has been implemented as parameterized neural networks.
The code for the joint training loss L = E[||η̂₀(α,β,I(α,β)) - x₀||² + ||η̂₁(α,β,I(α,β)) - x₁||²] has been implemented as per Equations 3 and 4.
The code enforces the consistency constraint x = α·η₀(α,β,x) + β·η₁(α,β,x) as given in Equation 5, either explicitly or by learning only one drift and deriving the other.
The agent has read Proposition 2.5 to understand that transporting samples along any path (αₜ,βₜ) is achieved by integrating the probability flow ODE given in Equation 7.
The agent has read Proposition 2.6 to understand the diffusion SDE formulation in Equation 8, extending the ODE with a noise term controlled by εₜ.
The plan includes implementing the probability flow ODE Ẋₜ = α̇ₜη₀(αₜ,βₜ,Xₜ) + β̇ₜη₁(αₜ,βₜ,Xₜ) for generation.
The plan includes implementing the SDE dXₜ = (α̇ₜ - εₜαₜ⁻¹)η₀dt + β̇ₜη₁dt + √(2εₜ)dWₜ for stochastic generation.
The code for the probability flow ODE (Equation 7) has been implemented, computing the drift from η₀ and η₁ and integrating with an Euler solver.
The code for the diffusion SDE (Equation 8) has been implemented, including the additional score correction term -εₜαₜ⁻¹η₀ and the Wiener noise term √(2εₜ)dWₜ.
The code for Algorithm 1 (Multitask learner) has been implemented: sampling batches of (x₀,x₁,α,β), computing interpolants, computing the combined loss, and performing gradient updates.
The code for Algorithm 2 (Multitask generator) has been implemented: initializing from I(α₀,β₀), iterating K Euler steps with the learned drifts η̂₀ and η̂₁ along a specified path (αₜ,βₜ).
The agent has read Section 3.1 to understand the Hadamard-product interpolant I(α) = α⊙x₀ + (1-α)⊙x₁ for self-supervised inpainting and sequential generation.
The plan includes implementing the Hadamard-product interpolant I(α) = α⊙x₀ + (1-α)⊙x₁ where α ∈ [0,1]^d is a vector of per-dimension mixing coefficients.
The plan includes implementing the drift learning objective for the Hadamard interpolant (Equation 11) with α uniformly sampled from [0,1]^d.
The code for the Hadamard-product interpolant I(α) = α⊙x₀ + (1-α)⊙x₁ has been implemented as per Equation 9.
The code for the drift η(α,x) = E[x₀ - x₁ | α⊙x₀ + (1-α)⊙x₁ = x] (Equation 10) has been implemented as a neural network target.
The code for the Hadamard drift training objective (Equation 11) has been implemented with α sampled uniformly from [0,1]^d.
The inpainting inference procedure has been implemented: setting α̇ᵢ = 0 for observed pixels (i ∈ σ) and αᵢ = 1-t for masked pixels (i ∈ σᶜ), with η zeroed at unmasked positions.
The agent has read Section 3.2 to understand the multichannel denoising setup using operators in the Fourier domain as in Equation 12.
The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand the posterior sampling formalism using quadratic rewards, including Proposition 3.1 and Equations 18-21.
The plan includes implementing posterior sampling via Proposition 3.1: computing the shifted coordinates xᵣ and modified pair (αᵣ,βᵣ) from the quadratic reward A and offset b.
The code for computing αᵣ and βᵣ satisfying the constraint in Equation 20 has been implemented for given reward operator A.
The code for computing the shifted coordinates xᵣ from Equation 21 has been implemented.
The code for the posterior drifts η₀ʳ (Equation 18) and η₁ʳ (Equation 19) has been implemented using the pretrained prior drifts without retraining.
The agent has read Section 4 and Algorithm 1 to understand the full training procedure including the distribution ν over (α,β) pairs.
The agent has read Appendix B.1 to check all hyperparameters and architecture details for the image inpainting experiments.
The plan includes the two-stage training strategy: first training on diagonal α (all entries equal), then fine-tuning on α uniformly distributed in [0,1]^d.
The plan includes fine-tuning a second variant for block-structured α where entries in each 4×4 block share the same value.
The plan includes training on the four datasets: MNIST (28×28), φ⁴ model (32×32), CelebA (128×128), and AFHQ-Cat (256×256) with their respective sizes and hyperparameters from Table 2.
The U-Net architecture from Ho et al. (2020) has been implemented as the backbone for all image generation experiments.
The U-Net dimension multipliers have been implemented correctly: [1,2,2,2] for MNIST and φ⁴, and [1,2,4,8] for CelebA and AFHQ-Cat, as per Table 2.
The Adam optimizer has been implemented with learning rate 0.0002 for MNIST and φ⁴ experiments, and 0.0001 for CelebA and AFHQ-Cat experiments.
The learning rate decay schedule (factor 0.995 per 1000 iterations, or factor of 2 every 1500 iterations) has been implemented during training.
The training uses batches of 30 draws from the target distribution and 50 time slices (operator samples) per batch.
The code for the two-stage training procedure has been implemented: stage 1 trains on scalar/diagonal α, stage 2 fine-tunes on full [0,1]^d hypercube α.
The MNIST dataset (60,000 training images, 28×28) loading and preprocessing pipeline has been implemented.
The CelebA dataset (190,000 training images, resized to 128×128) loading and preprocessing pipeline has been implemented.
The AFHQ-Cat dataset (5,000 training images, resized to 256×256) loading and preprocessing pipeline has been implemented.
The inpainting evaluation setup has been implemented: square masks of sizes 40×40 and 80×80 with Gaussian noise (σ=0.05) and random masks covering 70% of pixels with Gaussian noise (σ=0.01).
PSNR and SSIM metrics have been implemented for quantitative evaluation on 100 test images per dataset.
The training for the MNIST inpainting and sequential generation experiment has been executed successfully without errors.
The training for the CelebA inpainting experiment (4×10⁵ steps, 4 GPUs, batch size 128) has been executed successfully without errors.
The training for the AFHQ-Cat inpainting experiment (4×10⁵ steps, 4 GPUs, batch size 64) has been executed successfully without errors.
The evaluation of PSNR and SSIM on CelebA (random mask and block mask) has been executed successfully, producing quantitative metrics.
The evaluation of PSNR and SSIM on AFHQ-Cat (random mask and block mask) has been executed successfully, producing quantitative metrics.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the method achieves PSNR of 33.76 and SSIM of 0.967 on CelebA random mask inpainting.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the method achieves PSNR of 29.98 and SSIM of 0.938 on CelebA block mask inpainting.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the method achieves PSNR of 33.11 and SSIM of 0.945 on AFHQ-Cat random mask inpainting.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the method achieves PSNR of 26.96 and SSIM of 0.914 on AFHQ-Cat block mask inpainting.
The execution outcomes confirm that the method ranks first or second in PSNR/SSIM across all CelebA and AFHQ-Cat inpainting tasks compared to PnP-Flow, Flow-Priors, D-Flow, and OT-ODE baselines.
The agent has read Section 5.1 and Figure 3 to understand the MNIST sequential generation experiment: autoregressive, block-wise, and column-wise generation starting from the same initial noise.
The sequential (autoregressive) generation procedure has been implemented for MNIST: progressively updating the operator mask block-by-block with multiple forward passes.
The MNIST sequential generation experiment (autoregressive, block-wise, column-wise) has been executed successfully, producing images without retraining.
The execution outcomes qualitatively match Figure 3: inpainting of various corruptions and sequential generation in arbitrary orders from the same initial noise are visually plausible on MNIST.
The agent has read Section 5.2 and Appendix B.2 to understand the φ⁴ model posterior sampling experiment.
The plan includes implementing the φ⁴ energy function E(φ) = (χ/2)Σ|φ(a)-φ(b)|² + (κ/2)Σ|φ(a)|² + (γ/4)Σ|φ(a)|⁴ as per Equation 24.
The plan includes using MCMC sampling (Langevin SDE) to generate ground-truth φ⁴ configurations near the phase transition.
The φ⁴ model energy E(φ) (Equation 24) has been implemented on a 2D periodic lattice of size L×L (L=32).
The Fourier-domain decomposition of the energy E₀(φ) via the spectral operator M̂(k) (Equations 43, 44) has been implemented.
The Langevin SDE for MCMC sampling of φ⁴ configurations (Equation 47 discretized in Equation 48) has been implemented using FFT/IFFT operations.
The φ⁴ posterior sampling using Proposition B.3 (linear field h: shift φᵣ = φ + αα^T β^{-T} h and posterior drift Equation 57) has been implemented.
The stochastic interpolant model has been trained on 100,000 φ⁴ lattice configurations (32×32, batch size 100, 2×10⁵ steps) and the training run has completed successfully.
The posterior sampling from the φ⁴ model with applied field h=0.02 has been executed successfully without retraining.

The execution outcomes match Figure 5: the histogram of magnetization M(φ)=(1/N)Σφ(a) for 2000 generated configurations matches the MCMC ground truth for both the prior (h=0) and posterior (h=0.02) distributions.
The agent has read Section 5.3 and Appendix B.3 to understand the maze planning experiment, including the U-Net architecture and path-length heuristic.
The plan includes implementing the maze planning as a zero-shot inpainting problem: fixing trajectory endpoints with αᵢ=0 and generating the full path simultaneously using the Hadamard interpolant.
The plan includes using sparse paths of length 50 subsampled from length-300 paths (every 6 points), with linear interpolation to recover the full path at inference.
The U-Net for the maze experiment has been implemented with 4 encoding stages of 48, 80, 160, 256 channels with corresponding decoder and residual connections.

The maze U-Net input has been implemented with shape d×2 (trajectory length × xy-coordinates) and interpolation/max pooling applied independently per coordinate column to reduce only along the trajectory axis.
The maze U-Net convolution kernel of size 5×2 with padding of two rows at top and bottom (to maintain identical input/output dimensions) has been implemented.
The progressive path-length heuristic has been implemented: searching over candidate target indices [5,10,20,30,40,45,50] and selecting the shortest index that produces a valid (obstacle-free) path.
The maze training data pipeline has been implemented: randomly extracting paths of length 300 from a 2,000,000-step trajectory and subsampling every 6 points to create length-50 sparse paths.
The maze planning model has been trained successfully and the generation of 10,000 paths between randomly chosen point pairs has been executed.
The execution outcomes match Section 5.3: all 10,000 generated paths avoid forbidden maze regions, demonstrating 100% validity.
The execution outcomes match Figure 8: additional path constraints (e.g., forcing the path through the bottom-right corner) are correctly enforced by setting αᵢ=0 at the waypoint index.
The agent has read Appendix B.1 to check the two-stage fine-tuning procedure for block-structured α (4×4 blocks with shared values per block).
The block-structured α fine-tuning has been implemented: matrices αₜ decomposed into 4×4 blocks where all entries within a block share the same value sampled from [0,1].
The Euler integrator for solving the ODE/SDE during inference has been implemented, as specified in Appendix B.1.
The execution outcomes match Figure 4: inpainting results on CelebA and AFHQ-Cat are visually realistic and artifact-free, though with slight over-smoothing.
The φ⁴ model inpainting of three different configurations (Figure 7) has been executed, demonstrating conditional generation given partially observed lattice states.
